blocks.text: To the church of God that is in Corinth, to those who are sanctified in Christ Jesus, called to be saints, together with all those who in every place call on the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, both their Lord and ours:

2 : (Gk “ekklesia theou”) may be a translation of Heb (“assembly of Yahweh”) expressing a sense of divine election; but “ekklesia” was the designation of the city “assembly” of Corinth, so that may have evoked a sense of belonging to an alternative community.
2b : This phrase is likely a gloss added to the prescript in order to make Paul's advice applicable to , an indication that the original letter has been edited.
Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
1.1–3: Prescript and Greeting. The prescript of Paul's letters has the same form found in many papyrus letters of antiquity: the names of the sender and the addressees, with a short greeting.
3 : The greeting derives from older Jewish formulas (see 78.2), but embodies the essence of Paul's own experience of God in Christ.
I give thanks to my God always for you because of the grace of God that has been given you in Christ Jesus,
4 : Paul gives thanks for the to the Corinthians, not for their “labor of love” as in 1 Thess 1.3.
for in every way you have been enriched in him, in speech and knowledge of every kind—
5 : Because and are attainments of which the Corinthians are proud (1.17; 8.1–2; 13.1–2), Paul's selective mention of these gifts has an ironic quality, enhanced by the hyperbolic phrases,
just as the testimony of Christ has been strengthened among you—
so that you are not lacking in any spiritual gift as you wait for the revealing of our Lord Jesus Christ.
He will also strengthen you to the end, so that you may be blameless on the day of our Lord Jesus Christ.
8 : Paul's assurance that God the Corinthians contains a mild reproach of their instability. is an apocalyptic term for the day of judgment; see 3.13; 4.5.
God is faithful; by him you were called into the fellowship of his Son, Jesus Christ our Lord.
1.4–9: Thanksgiving. The thanksgiving is a standard feature of the prescripts of Paul's letters, comparable to the assurance of prayer for the addressees of other ancient letters.

Now I appeal to you, brothers and sisters, by the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, that all of you be in agreement and that there be no divisions among you, but that you be united in the same mind and the same purpose.
For it has been reported to me by Chloe's people that there are quarrels among you, my brothers and sisters.
11 : Mention of without introduction indicates that she and her (probably slaves) were known to the Corinthians and that she probably lived in Corinth.
What I mean is that each of you says, “I belong to Paul,” or “I belong to Apollos,” or “I belong to Cephas,” or “I belong to Christ.”

12 : Paul caricatures the Corinthians’ partisanship using slogans like those shouted by fans of star performers in the amphitheater. , from Alexandria in Egypt, was an eloquent early Christian preacher according to Acts 18.24–28; see also 1 Cor 3.4–6; 4.6; 16.12. , the Aramaic form of Peter's name. The climactic slogan, , is added for ironic effect, and does not indicate the existence of a “Christ-party.”
Has Christ been divided? Was Paul crucified for you? Or were you baptized in the name of Paul?
13 : Paul's rhetorical question, uses a verb related to a common Greek term for “party”: “Has the body of Christ been split into parties?”
I thank God that I baptized none of you except Crispus and Gaius,

14 : Paul's thanksgiving that he has a note of irony which suggests that Crispus and Gaius may have been involved in forming factions. is the former synagogue president (Acts 18.8), while is described as “the host … to the whole church” (Rom 16.23); both are evidently of high social standing.
so that no one can say that you were baptized in my name.
(I did baptize also the household of Stephanas; beyond that, I do not know whether I baptized anyone else.)
16 : Paul employs the device of a feigned lapse of memory to separate , his strongest supporter (see 16.15–18), from Crispus and Gaius.
For Christ did not send me to baptize but to proclaim the gospel, and not with eloquent wisdom, so that the cross of Christ might not be emptied of its power.

1.10–17: Divisions in the church. Paul describes the situation in the church of Corinth in terms also used by political orators to characterize conflicts within city-states: and are synonyms for discord, and Paul's appeal to and echoes the language of speeches on concord.

17 : Paul formulates his commission as an apostle antithetically: . The negative definition of the style and content of Paul's preaching ( ) suggests that the Corinthians have found Paul's proclamation deficient.
For the message about the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God.

18 : Paul provocatively reduces the content of the gospel to a single, shameful event— . Greek “moria” ( ) has a social stigma that does not attach to English “folly”: better, “a vulgar joke.” , lit. “the word of the cross.”
For it is written, “I will destroy the wisdom of the wise, and the discernment of the discerning I will thwart.”
19 : Isa 29.14 (LXX). This text is particularly apt, since it critiques those who take pride in wisdom and foretells the creation of a new order in which the deaf will hear and the blind will see.
Where is the one who is wise? Where is the scribe? Where is the debater of this age? Has not God made foolish the wisdom of the world?

20 : Paul's rhetorical questions mock the educated elite by means of culturally specific titles of dignity: the Jewish is not merely a skilled copyist but an expert in the scriptures; the is a philosophical researcher highly esteemed in the Hellenic world.
For since, in the wisdom of God, the world did not know God through wisdom, God decided, through the foolishness of our proclamation, to save those who believe.
21 : Paul shapes a playful contrast between two types of : on the one hand, a divine attribute; on the other, a human attainment (cf. Sir 1.9–10; Wis 7; 8).
For Jews demand signs and Greeks desire wisdom,
22 : The for among the was proverbial: see Herodotus 4.77.1.
but we proclaim Christ crucified, a stumbling block to Jews and foolishness to Gentiles,

23 : The first-century orator Cicero attests the constraint upon discourse about the cross among persons of higher social class: “The mere mention of the word ‘cross’ is shameful to a Roman citizen and a free man” ( 5.16).
but to those who are the called, both Jews and Greeks, Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God.
For God's foolishness is wiser than human wisdom, and God's weakness is stronger than human strength.
24–25 : In formulating the paradox of the as and , Paul employs phrases that sound positively blasphemous— .
Consider your own call, brothers and sisters: not many of you were wise by human standards, not many were powerful, not many were of noble birth.
But God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise; God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong;
God chose what is low and despised in the world, things that are not, to reduce to nothing things that are,

26–28 : The terms used by Paul to describe the makeup of the community are those employed by Greek writers to designate the major class divisions in civil strife: , and are euphemisms for “the rich,” while “the poor” are the , and (born). Paul offers a radical explanation of why the majority of those who have been “called” by the gospel of the crucified are persons lacking in education, wealth, and birth: (repeated three times for emphasis).
so that no one might boast in the presence of God.
He is the source of your life in Christ Jesus, who became for us wisdom from God, and righteousness and sanctification and redemption,
in order that, as it is written, “Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord.”
1.18–2.5: The folly of the message about the cross. Paul acknowledges that his preaching is to the educated elite, but asserts that God has confounded the of this world.
26–31 : Paul expounds the social consequences of the divine reversal of wisdom and foolishness.
